name: Cáceres-Puerto del Pico-Cáceres Route
name_original: Dolmen de las Hijadillas II — circular desde el Castillo palacio de las Seguras
description: This 9.0-mile jogging route near Cáceres offers a moderate challenge with 356 feet of elevation gain, taking about 1 hour 45 minutes.
region_intro: Jogging around Cáceres offers diverse opportunities to explore its unique natural landscapes and historic urban areas. The region features a blend of well-maintained urban parks and significant protected natural spaces, providing varied terrains for running. Runners can experience routes through distinctive granite rock formations, Mediterranean vegetation, and gentle hills. The area's rich natural heritage complements its historic city center, making it an appealing destination for outdoor pursuits. Best jogging routes around Cáceres The most popular jogging route is Vía de la Plata: Cáceres Historic Center Loop, a 3.5 miles (5.6 km) trail that takes about 35 minutes to complete. This route offers a unique experience through the city's historic core. Another top favourite among local runners is Mining and Hermitages Route — circular from Cáceres, a moderate 6.1 miles (9.8 km) path. This route explores the varied terrain outside the city, including natural and historical points of interest. Local runners also love the Portanchito and Sanctuary of the Mountain Trail, a 5.1 miles (8.2 km) trail leading through an ascent to the Sanctuary of the Virgen de la Montaña, often completed in about 55 minutes. Jogging around Cáceres is defined by urban green spaces, protected natural areas with granite formations, and routes offering rewarding views from local hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy park loops to more challenging ascents. The routes in Cáceres are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.9 stars from over 15 reviews. More than 1400 runners have used komoot to explore Cáceres's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Cáceres?
A: Cáceres offers a wide selection of running routes, with over 130 different trails available for exploration. These routes cater to various fitness levels, ranging from easy strolls to more challenging runs.

Q: Are there easy running paths for beginners or casual joggers in Cáceres?
A: Yes, Cáceres provides several easy running paths perfect for beginners or those looking for a relaxed jog. While the majority are moderate, there are at least 3 routes specifically rated as easy. You can find pleasant green spaces within the city, such as Parque de El Príncipe and Olivar de los Frailes, which offer accessible paths.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running trails around Cáceres?
A: The running trails around Cáceres feature diverse terrain. You'll encounter urban green spaces, paths through distinctive granite rock formations, and areas with Mediterranean vegetation. Some routes include gentle hills, while others offer flatter sections, providing a varied experience for runners.

Q: Can I find routes with scenic views or natural landmarks?
A: Absolutely! Many routes offer rewarding views and pass by significant natural landmarks. For an uphill challenge with panoramic vistas, consider the ascent to the Sanctuary of the Virgen de la Montaña, which is part of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345586731/portanchito-and-sanctuary-of-the-mountain-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portanchito and Sanctuary of the Mountain Trail&lt;/a&gt;. The nearby &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6093807" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Barruecos Natural Monument&lt;/a&gt; is also renowned for its unique granite formations and large white stork colonies, offering picturesque running opportunities.

Q: Are there any family-friendly running routes in Cáceres?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' designations aren't always explicit, urban parks like Parque de El Príncipe and Parque de El Cuartillo offer green spaces and established paths that are generally suitable for families. These areas provide a safe and pleasant environment for a jog or walk with children.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the running trails in Cáceres?
A: Many natural areas and urban parks in Cáceres are dog-friendly, though it's always advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially in protected areas or where wildlife is present. For specific routes, checking local signage or community comments on komoot tours can provide the most up-to-date information on dog policies.

Q: Are there circular running routes available?
A: Yes, many of the running routes in Cáceres are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1343774062/mining-and-hermitages-route-circular-from-caceres" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mining and Hermitages Route — circular from Cáceres&lt;/a&gt;, which explores varied terrain outside the city. Another is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345655929/parque-del-principe-y-olivar-de-los-frailes-circular-por-el-cerro-otero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque del Príncipe y Olivar de los Frailes — circular por el Cerro Otero&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions near the jogging routes?
A: Beyond the routes themselves, Cáceres is surrounded by impressive natural attractions. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6093807" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Barruecos Natural Monument&lt;/a&gt;, known for its granite rock formations and storks, is a must-see. You can also find beautiful water features like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4396799" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lower Barrueco Pond&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4343940" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadiloba Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; nearby, offering scenic backdrops for your runs.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Cáceres?
A: The running routes in Cáceres are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 3.9 stars from over 15 reviews. Runners often praise the varied terrain, the blend of urban and natural landscapes, and the rewarding views from local hills. Over 1400 runners have already used komoot to explore the area.

Q: Are there challenging running routes for experienced runners?
A: Yes, for experienced runners seeking a challenge, Cáceres offers a good selection of difficult routes. Approximately 34 routes are rated as difficult, featuring more significant elevation changes and potentially more technical terrain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345637822/paseo-alto-y-la-sierrilla-circular-desde-el-estadio-principe-felipe" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Paseo Alto y la Sierrilla — circular desde el Estadio Príncipe Felipe&lt;/a&gt; is an example of a more challenging option.

Q: What is the best time of year to go jogging in Cáceres?
A: Cáceres offers good jogging conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn are particularly pleasant, with mild temperatures and lush landscapes. Summer can be warm, but routes with shade or early morning/late evening runs are enjoyable. The region's diverse natural features, including woodlands and natural swimming pools in areas like Sierra de Gata, suggest that different seasons offer unique experiences.

Q: How accessible are the running routes by public transport or car parking?
A: Within Cáceres city, many urban parks and starting points for routes are generally accessible by public transport or offer nearby parking. For routes further afield, such as those near Monfragüe National Park or Los Barruecos, car access is often the most convenient option, with designated parking areas available at popular trailheads. It's recommended to check specific route details for parking information.

Q: Can I find running routes that explore the historic parts of Cáceres?
A: Yes, you can combine your run with a glimpse into Cáceres's rich history. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345914672/via-de-la-plata-caceres-historic-center-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía de la Plata: Cáceres Historic Center Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a unique experience through the city's historic core, allowing you to jog past ancient walls and historic buildings. While the Old Town is primarily for leisurely exploration, this route provides a taste of its unique atmosphere.
tips: A 14th century defensive castle was built to protect Cáceres and the trade routes to and from Badajoz. Today, the palace is the private residence of the Viscount of Torre Hidalgo and the lush gardens are rented for events.

The ruins of this hermitage, also known as El Salvador, are very close to the Mogollones tower.
Originally, it was a cistern of the castle to which it belonged, which later became a hermitage. It houses outstanding mural paintings.

It is well known that the Barruecos area is rich in archaeological sites of all kinds, and that a Chalcolithic settlement is located in it, therefore the archaeological wealth that we also find nearby is not surprising, in La Hijadilla, located immediately after passing the Salor River, where a famous megalithic complex made up of dolmens is located, of which only Hijadillas is known

Through this stone bridge with several arches we will cross the Salor River. If we look closely, to the east we can see the remains of the Hijadilla mill which, although it is a building present since medieval times, was built in 1862.

faqs.question: Is the Cáceres-Puerto del Pico-Cáceres Route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The komoot route, which is approximately 14.5 km long with moderate elevation changes, is classified as moderate. This makes it suitable for joggers with a reasonable level of fitness. However, it's important to note that a continuous, conceptual jogging route from Cáceres all the way to Puerto del Pico and back would be an extremely challenging, multi-day endeavor, requiring exceptional fitness and experience.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this jogging route?
faqs.answer: The terrain near Cáceres, where this route is located, offers a mix of surfaces. You can expect dirt paths and rural roads, potentially passing through agricultural land and Mediterranean forests. While the route is designed for jogging, some sections might be more rugged than typical urban paths.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this jogging route?
faqs.answer: Based on the route's distance and elevation, it typically takes around 1 hour and 45 minutes to complete this jogging route. This duration can vary depending on your pace and any stops you make along the way.

faqs.question: Where does the Cáceres-Puerto del Pico-Cáceres Route start?
faqs.answer: The komoot route is a circular path starting and ending in Cáceres. While the exact starting point isn't specified, you can typically find parking within or near the city center to access local trails. For specific trailhead parking, check the route details on the komoot app or website.

faqs.question: What points of interest can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers several interesting sights. You can pass by the historic Castillo Palacio de las Seguras, the serene Hermitage of San Jorge, the ancient Dolmen de Hijadilla, and the picturesque Stone Bridge over the Salor River.

faqs.question: Is the Cáceres-Puerto del Pico-Cáceres Route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, trails in the Cáceres region are dog-friendly, especially rural paths. However, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash, particularly near agricultural areas, livestock, or in more populated sections. Be sure to carry water for your dog, especially during warmer months.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to jog this route is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder and more pleasant for outdoor activities. Summers can be very hot in Extremadura, making early morning or late evening runs preferable, while winters are generally mild but can be damp.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to jog this route. It utilizes public paths and rural roads around Cáceres.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other established paths. Notably, it covers about 50% of the Camino Natural Corredor Cáceres-Badajoz. Ruta Cáceres-Puerto del Pico-Cáceres, and also intersects with parts of the Camino Natural del Corredor Cáceres - Badajoz and the Vereda de Malpartida o del Lavadero de Lanas.

faqs.question: What should I bring for jogging this route?
faqs.answer: For this moderate jogging route, you should bring appropriate running shoes, comfortable athletic clothing, and sufficient water, especially on warmer days. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen) is also advisable. A phone for navigation and emergencies is always a good idea.

faqs.question: Is this a loop trail or an out-and-back route?
faqs.answer: This specific komoot route is designed as a circular loop, starting and ending in Cáceres. This means you will return to your starting point without retracing your steps.

waypoints.name: Stone Bridge over the Salor River
